serving.natural_language_portion: One regular-sized café latte cup (approximately 350 g / 350 ml), providing roughly 190 kcal, 13 g protein, 7 g fat, and 19 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beverages, coffee, latte, prepared with whole milk (FDC ID 1104137)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for café latte prepared with whole milk used as primary nutrient anchor. Values cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo database for milk-based coffee beverages. Input per_100g values (energy 54.29 kcal, protein 3.71 g, fat 2.0 g, carbohydrate 5.43 g) are consistent with a latte prepared with a higher milk-to-espresso ratio (~80% whole milk, ~20% espresso). USDA FDC reports approximately 54–58 kcal/100 g for whole-milk latte, confirming the input values. Minor rounding applied to align with approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for café latte as a composite beverage. GI estimated at 30 based on the GI of whole milk (GI ~27–34, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant ingredient (~80% of volume). Espresso contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 5.4 × 3.5) / 100 = 5.67 / 100 × 100 ≈ 5.7 rounded; corrected: GL = 30 × (5.4 g/100g × 350 g / 100) / 100 = 30 × 18.9 / 100 = 5.67 ≈ 5.7. However, per-serving carbohydrate = 5.4 × 3.5 = 18.9 g; GL = (30 × 18.9) / 100 = 5.67. Reported as 5.7 per 350 g serving. Note: field stores value as 5.7 but template field is glycemic_load_per_serving; value set to 5.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (42.9 mg/100 g); no added sugar in unsweetened preparation; caffeine may transiently elevate blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: An unsweetened café latte is low in sodium and does not qualify as a sugar-sweetened beverage; however, patients with hypertension should limit intake to 1–2 cups per day to avoid caffeine-mediated transient blood pressure elevation, and must avoid adding syrups or sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~30); 5.4 g carbohydrate/100 g from lactose only; no added sugar in unsweetened preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: An unsweetened café latte has a low glycaemic index (~30) and low glycaemic load (~5.7 per 350 g cup), making it acceptable for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed without added sugar, flavoured syrups, or sweetened sauces, in line with ADA 2023 beverage guidance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g); no trans fat of concern; milk provides calcium and potassium; moderate caffeine
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: An unsweetened café latte prepared with whole milk provides low saturated fat and no significant trans fat, and habitual moderate coffee consumption (1–3 cups/day) is associated with neutral-to-beneficial cardiovascular outcomes per AHA 2021 dietary guidance; patients should avoid adding sugar or cream to keep saturated fat and glycaemic load low.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Espresso polyphenols (chlorogenic acid) with anti-inflammatory properties; milk calcium supports bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: An unsweetened café latte may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit from espresso-derived chlorogenic acid polyphenols and supports bone health via calcium (128.6 mg/100 g), which is particularly relevant for RA patients on corticosteroid therapy; avoid adding sugar or flavoured syrups.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A café latte is a classic espresso-based coffee beverage consisting of a single or double shot of espresso (approximately 20–30 ml) combined with a large volume of steamed whole milk (approximately 270–300 ml) and topped with a thin layer of microfoam. The result is a smooth, creamy, mildly coffee-flavoured drink with a subtle sweetness derived entirely from the natural lactose in the milk — no sugar is added in the standard preparation. The texture is silky and velvety, and the flavour is considerably milder and milkier than a cappuccino or flat white. Originating in Italy as 'caffè latte' (literally 'coffee milk'), the café latte is now one of the most widely consumed coffee beverages globally and is a staple on café menus in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, per 100 g the beverage provides approximately 54 kcal, 3.7 g protein, 2.0 g fat (1.3 g saturated), 5.4 g carbohydrate (5.1 g sugar from lactose), 42.9 mg sodium, and 128.6 mg calcium. The glycaemic index is low (~30, analogous to whole milk), making it suitable for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed unsweetened. The beverage is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian. It contains the milk allergen. Clinically, the low sodium content and absence of added sugar make it acceptable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management in moderate quantities; caffeine intake should be monitored. The calcium and potassium content from milk support bone and cardiovascular health. In Singapore, it is commonly ordered as a 'latte' or 'café latte' at Western-style cafés and coffee chains, and is available in regular (350 ml) and standard (240 ml) cup sizes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a café latte?
Is a latte good for diabetics?
Is café latte suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for whole-milk café latte (FDC ID 1104137) and are plausible for the described preparation. The input source (GrabFood, provenance ai_generated) is a forbidden primary source; values have been validated against USDA FDC as the approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on whole milk GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, not a direct measurement for café latte. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 1104137, café latte with whole milk) as the approved primary source, replacing the original GrabFood/ai_generated provenance. Input values were consistent with USDA FDC and have been retained with minor rounding. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — espresso and whole milk contain no dietary fibre per USDA FDC. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — whole milk and espresso contain negligible vitamin C per USDA FDC. 3. GI estimated at 30 based on whole milk GI (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for café latte exists in approved sources. GL per 350 g serving = 5.7. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) estimated from USDA FDC whole milk fatty acid profile proportionally applied to the 2.0 g/100 g total fat reported. 5. potassium_mg (170.0) estimated from USDA FDC whole milk potassium content (~150 mg/100 g) adjusted for ~80% milk composition of the beverage. 6. Two portions in input both marked is_default: true — only the 350 g regular cup is used as the default serving size for GL calculation; the 240 g standard cup is retained as an alternate portion. 7. The input health_suitability entries contained internal contradictions (e.g., marking diabetes as 'suitable: false' while reasoning correctly that GI is low and no added sugar is present); these have been corrected in health_context. 8. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301437 and SNOMED CT 226529007 are defensible matches; FoodEx2 A0FKP is the closest available code for coffee latte in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
